refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligation—enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelope—without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The actual achievement of p95 ≤ CT_target is a verification criterion, not a functional requirement. This separation allows DPs to address enablement independently of operational performance measurement.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that under nominal duty cycle and site conditions, the NGHE solution's design permits completion of load→travel→dump within CT_target. Measure p95 cycle time across representative operational scenarios; if p95 ≤ CT_target is consistently achieved, the FR is satisfied. If not, root-cause analysis determines whether the failure is in DP (design does not enable it) or in IM/operational factors (design is sound but execution/training/site conditions prevent it).
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall enable the load phase to complete within the allocated time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall enable the travel phase to complete within the allocated time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall enable the dump phase to complete within the allocated time budget.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose the load→travel→dump cycle into sequential sub-operations (load, travel, dump) with independent timing budgets that sum to CT_target, each optimized for its operational constraints.
DP2: Implement a centralized cycle controller that orchestrates all sub-operations via state machine logic, with feedback loops to detect and compensate for delays in real-time.

DP3: Design the powertrain, hydraulics, and actuation systems to deliver peak performance margins (e.g., 15–20% headroom above nominal duty) such that nominal cycle time naturally stays within CT_target under site conditions.

DP4: Establish a hierarchical timing architecture: global cycle supervisor monitors p95 performance; sub-operation controllers manage local timing; adaptive throttling adjusts load/travel/dump rates based on measured cycle history.

DP5: Decouple cycle timing from external variability (e.g., load mass, terrain, operator skill) by implementing load-adaptive and terrain-adaptive sub-operation strategies that maintain consistent cycle time across the duty cycle envelope.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4 (Hierarchical Timing Architecture with Adaptive Throttling) combined with DP5 (Load/Terrain Adaptation) and DP3 (Performance Margin Design). Rationale: DP4 provides observable, controllable timing at multiple levels; DP5 decouples cycle time from external variability; DP3 ensures physical feasibility. Together they form a robust, measurable solution that directly addresses the p95 ≤ CT_target acceptance criterion.

potential_sources: Coupling between powertrain margin (DP3) and cycle controller logic (DP4): if margins are insufficient, controller cannot compensate; requires co-design of hardware and control strategy.
Coupling between load-adaptive strategy (DP5) and sensor/estimation subsystem: accurate load/terrain sensing is prerequisite; if sensing fails, adaptation degrades.

Coupling between sub-operation timing budgets (DP1) and actual site conditions: budgets must be validated under real duty cycle; mismatch between lab assumptions and field conditions risks p95 violation.
Coupling between operator behavior and cycle timing: if operator deviates from standard procedure, timing guarantees may not hold; requires training and procedural enforcement.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP set exhibits multiple sources of coupling that must be resolved through explicit co-design, validation, and operational constraints. These couplings are not inherent flaws but require active management and cannot be treated as independent design choices.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Powertrain and hydraulic systems can be designed to deliver 15–20% performance margin without excessive cost or weight penalty. Risk: If margin is unachievable, DP3 fails and cycle time becomes sensitive to wear, temperature, and load variation.

Assumption: Load mass, terrain type, and operator skill can be estimated or measured with sufficient accuracy to enable adaptive throttling. Risk: If estimation is poor, DP5 provides false confidence; p95 may still be violated.

Assumption: Site conditions (temperature, humidity, terrain roughness, fuel quality) remain within the agreed duty cycle envelope. Risk: Outlier conditions (e.g., extreme cold, muddy terrain) may invalidate timing budgets.

Assumption: Cycle controller can be implemented with sufficient determinism and latency to enforce timing without introducing jitter. Risk: Software/firmware delays or race conditions could undermine p95 guarantee.

Assumption: Operator training and worksite procedures enforce consistent cycle execution. Risk: Operator variability (e.g., aggressive loading, excessive idling) could violate timing targets despite correct DP design.

Assumption: Measurement and validation of p95 cycle time can be performed reliably in the field (e.g., via onboard telemetry or manual timing). Risk: If measurement is unreliable, acceptance criteria cannot be verified.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine the FR to explicitly state: (1) the duty cycle envelope (load mass range, terrain types, ambient conditions, operator skill level); (2) the measurement method and validation protocol for p95 cycle time; (3) the acceptable sources of variability (e.g., ±5% tolerance on CT_target due to site conditions). This will enable tighter DP specification and reduce risk of post-delivery disputes. Additionally, decompose DP4 and DP5 into separate FR→DP pairs at the next level: FR4a (Detect and log cycle timing and sub-operation delays) → DP4a (Onboard telemetry and state machine instrumentation); FR5a (Adapt sub-operation rates to load and terrain) → DP5a (Load estimation and terrain classification logic). This separation clarifies ownership boundaries and enables independent testing of timing control vs. adaptation logic.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates hierarchical timing control, adaptive throttling logic, load/terrain sensing, performance margin enforcement, and cycle-time regulation at the platform level. Owns the real-time scheduling substrate, sensor fusion for terrain/load classification, and actuator command throttling.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle exports cycle-time metrics, throttle state, terrain classification, and performance margin utilization to enable external monitoring and validation of p95 ≤ CT_target.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives mission commands and target cycle-time constraints from Teleoperation or autonomous planner; adapts internal throttling and load-balancing to meet declared targets.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives safety constraints (e.g., max acceleration, terrain hazards) and reports margin violations or throttling state changes that affect safety envelope.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives real-time updates on external load conditions, terrain maps, or network latency estimates that inform adaptive throttling decisions.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. DP4 (hierarchical timing + adaptive throttling), DP5 (load/terrain adaptation), and DP3 (performance margin) are all platform-level concerns. Vehicle owns sensors, actuators, real-time scheduler, and control loops. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces require external coordination, but logic remains internal.

Poor fit. NGHE is a system-level orchestrator, not a real-time control substrate. DP4 (timing architecture) and DP5 (load adaptation) require low-latency sensor feedback and actuator control that NGHE cannot provide. Timing logic would diffuse across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Violates cohesion principle.

Weak fit. Teleoperation is a command/feedback interface layer, not a control substrate. DP4 requires real-time hierarchical scheduling on the platform; DP5 requires onboard terrain/load sensing. Throttling logic would split between Teleoperation (command interpretation) and Vehicle (execution), creating tight coupling and latency risk.

Weak fit. Safety Services enforces constraints and monitors margins, but does not own the timing architecture or adaptive control loops. DP3 (performance margin design) overlaps with Safety Services' concern, but DP4 and DP5 are platform-level dynamics that Safety Services cannot directly control. Would require bidirectional diffusion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the natural owner of DP4 + DP5 + DP3 because it is the only module that owns the real-time execution substrate (scheduler, sensors, actuators, control loops) required to implement hierarchical timing, adaptive throttling, and performance margin enforcement. All three DPs operate on platform-level state and must respond to sub-millisecond feedback; centralizing them in Vehicle minimizes latency, coupling, and diffusion. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) allow other modules to set constraints and observe behavior without owning the control logic.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle module is not equipped with a real-time scheduler or deterministic timing substrate, DP4 (hierarchical timing architecture) cannot be realized. Mitigation: Verify Vehicle has RTOS or equivalent; if not, revise DP4 to defer timing hierarchy to a separate real-time kernel module.

Risk: If terrain/load sensing is distributed across multiple subsystems (e.g., wheel encoders in drivetrain, IMU in navigation), DP5 (load/terrain adaptation) may require sensor fusion logic outside Vehicle. Mitigation: Ensure Vehicle owns or aggregates all load/terrain sensors; if not, create a dedicated Sensor Fusion module and link it via inbound interface.

Risk: Safety Services may need to override or veto throttling decisions in real-time. If Safety Services is not tightly coupled to Vehicle's control loop, latency may violate safety margins. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert as a synchronous, low-latency interface; consider co-locating safety monitor logic with Vehicle or using shared-memory IPC.

Risk: Adaptive throttling based on Network Link latency estimates may create feedback loops that destabilize cycle time. Mitigation: Implement hysteresis and rate-limiting in DP4's throttling logic; validate stability via simulation before deployment.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. DP4 + DP5 + DP3 are well-suited to Vehicle containment. However, recommend clarifying the Vehicle module's internal architecture: explicitly separate the real-time control substrate (scheduler, throttling logic, margin enforcement) from the command/telemetry interface layer. This ensures DP4, DP5, and DP3 are realized as cohesive, low-latency subsystems within Vehicle, with clear boundaries to Tasking, Telemetry, and SafetyAlert interfaces. If Vehicle currently lacks a deterministic timing substrate, propose a revision to DP4 to specify the required RTOS or kernel-level scheduling mechanism as a prerequisite.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 (Hierarchical Timing Architecture with Adaptive Throttling), DP5 (Load/Terrain Adaptation), and DP3 (Performance Margin Design) directly and measurably address the FR requirement to enable load→travel→dump cycle execution within CT_target. The combination is logically sound: DP4 provides observable, controllable timing at multiple levels; DP5 decouples cycle time from external variability through adaptive control; DP3 ensures physical feasibility and margin enforcement. Together they form a complete solution that maps to the p95 ≤ CT_target acceptance criterion. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: SafetyAlert interface creates bidirectional coupling: Vehicle must report margin violations in real-time, and Safety Services must veto throttling decisions synchronously. If SafetyAlert latency exceeds ~10 ms, margin enforcement may fail. Mitigation: Specify SafetyAlert as a low-latency, synchronous interface; consider co-locating safety monitor logic with Vehicle's control loop or using shared-memory IPC.

Network Link interface introduces potential feedback instability: adaptive throttling based on latency estimates may create oscillatory behavior if rate-limiting and hysteresis are not carefully tuned. Mitigation: Implement hysteresis and rate-limiting in DP4's throttling logic; validate stability via closed-loop simulation before deployment.

Terrain/load sensing diffusion risk: if wheel encoders, IMU, or load cells are distributed across drivetrain, navigation, and payload subsystems, DP5 (load/terrain adaptation) may require sensor fusion logic outside Vehicle's direct ownership. Mitigation: Ensure Vehicle owns or aggregates all load/terrain sensors via a dedicated Sensor Fusion submodule; if not, create explicit Sensor Fusion module and link via inbound interface.

Tasking interface requires Vehicle to interpret mission commands and translate them into internal throttling/load-balancing parameters. If command semantics are ambiguous or if Vehicle lacks a command parser, diffusion risk increases. Mitigation: Define Tasking interface schema explicitly; ensure Vehicle owns command interpretation logic.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Vehicle module achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. Excellent fit: DP4 (hierarchical timing + adaptive throttling), DP5 (load/terrain adaptation), and DP3 (performance margin) are all platform-level concerns requiring real-time execution substrate, sensor feedback, and actuator control. Vehicle owns the real-time scheduler, sensors, actuators, and control loops necessary to realize all three DPs. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) allow constraint-setting and observation without diffusing control logic. Minor diffusion arises from SafetyAlert and Tasking coordination, but logic remains internal to Vehicle. NGHE (0.45 containment, 0.55 diffusion), Teleoperation (0.35 containment, 0.65 diffusion), and Safety Services (0.4 containment, 0.6 diffusion) are poor fits because they lack the real-time execution substrate and sensor/actuator ownership required by DP4 and DP5.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions' is directly measurable via Vehicle's Telemetry interface, which exports cycle-time metrics, throttle state, terrain classification, and performance margin utilization. Verification requires: (1) instrumentation of Vehicle to log cycle-time samples during field trials; (2) statistical analysis of p95 latency under representative duty cycles; (3) validation that margin violations are reported via SafetyAlert. All three are feasible within Vehicle's scope.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. Vehicle is the unambiguous owner of DP4, DP5, and DP3 realization. Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Network Link interfaces define clear boundaries for external constraint-setting and observation. However, recommend explicit clarification of Vehicle's internal architecture: separate the real-time control substrate (scheduler, throttling logic, margin enforcement) from the command/telemetry interface layer to ensure DP4, DP5, and DP3 are realized as cohesive, low-latency subsystems. If Vehicle currently lacks a deterministic timing substrate (RTOS or kernel-level scheduling), this must be identified as a prerequisite and documented in DP4 specification.

recommended_revisions: Clarify Vehicle module's internal architecture: explicitly separate real-time control substrate (scheduler, throttling logic, margin enforcement) from command/telemetry interface layer. Document the RTOS or kernel-level scheduling mechanism required to realize DP4 (Hierarchical Timing Architecture). If Vehicle lacks this substrate, revise DP4 to specify the required timing kernel as a prerequisite.

Define SafetyAlert interface as synchronous, low-latency (target: <10 ms round-trip). Specify the mechanism for Safety Services to veto throttling decisions in real-time. If synchronous coupling is infeasible, revise DP3 (Performance Margin Design) to include a safety margin buffer that accommodates asynchronous safety feedback.

Consolidate terrain/load sensing under Vehicle ownership or create a dedicated Sensor Fusion submodule with clear inbound interfaces to Vehicle. Ensure DP5 (Load/Terrain Adaptation) does not diffuse across multiple subsystems. Document sensor aggregation logic and latency budget.

Specify Tasking interface schema and command interpretation logic owned by Vehicle. Define how mission commands (e.g., target cycle time, load constraints) are translated into internal throttling and load-balancing parameters. Ensure Vehicle owns this translation logic to avoid diffusion to Teleoperation.

Implement hysteresis and rate-limiting in DP4's throttling logic to prevent oscillatory behavior from Network Link latency feedback. Validate closed-loop stability via simulation before deployment. Document stability margins and tuning parameters.

Add explicit verification plan to acceptance criteria: specify instrumentation, logging, and statistical analysis methods for measuring p95 ≤ CT_target. Ensure Telemetry interface exports sufficient granularity (cycle-time samples, throttle state, margin utilization) to enable post-hoc analysis and root-cause investigation of any p95 violations.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and the Vehicle module achieves excellent containment (0.92). However, the gate results expose critical architectural and specification gaps that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. Specifically: (1) Vehicle's internal architecture lacks explicit separation between real-time control substrate and command/telemetry interface, creating ambiguity about DP4 realization; (2) SafetyAlert interface coupling is bidirectional and synchronous, but latency budget (<10 ms) is not yet validated against Vehicle's actual control-loop cycle time; (3) Sensor Fusion logic for DP5 (Load/Terrain Adaptation) is not yet assigned to a clear module owner, risking diffusion across drivetrain, navigation, and payload subsystems; (4) Tasking interface schema and command interpretation logic are not yet specified, creating risk of diffusion to Teleoperation. These gaps are architectural, not functional—they do not invalidate the FR-DP mapping, but they must be resolved to ensure DP realization is feasible and contained. Proceeding to sub-FR decomposition without addressing these gaps would propagate ambiguity into lower levels and increase integration risk. Given high safety criticality, recommend REVISE_CURRENT_LEVEL to clarify Vehicle's internal architecture, interface contracts, and module ownership before deriving sub-FRs.

revise.why: DP4 (Hierarchical Timing Architecture with Adaptive Throttling), DP5 (Load/Terrain Adaptation), and DP3 (Performance Margin Design) are logically sound and correctly address FR, but their realization specifications are incomplete. Revisions required: (1) DP4 must explicitly specify the real-time control substrate (RTOS, kernel-level scheduler, or equivalent) and its cycle-time guarantees. If Vehicle lacks this substrate, DP4 must be revised to include substrate provisioning as a prerequisite. (2) DP5 must clarify sensor ownership and fusion logic: either Vehicle owns all load/terrain sensors and aggregates them internally, or a dedicated Sensor Fusion module is created with explicit inbound interfaces to Vehicle. (3) DP3 must specify SafetyAlert interface as synchronous with <10 ms latency budget, and include a safety margin buffer to accommodate worst-case latency. (4) All three DPs must include explicit verification criteria and instrumentation requirements to enable measurement of p95 ≤ CT_target. Recommend revising DP specifications to include: (a) internal architecture diagram showing real-time control substrate, throttling logic, margin enforcement, and command/telemetry interface layer; (b) interface contracts (Tasking, SafetyAlert, Telemetry, Network Link) with latency budgets and synchronization semantics; (c) sensor aggregation and fusion logic ownership; (d) closed-loop stability analysis for adaptive throttling; (e) verification plan with instrumentation and statistical analysis methods.

assumptions: Vehicle module has or can provision a deterministic real-time execution substrate (RTOS or kernel-level scheduler) capable of enforcing hierarchical timing with <10 ms control-loop cycle time.
SafetyAlert interface can be implemented as a synchronous, low-latency channel (target: <10 ms round-trip) without violating Safety Services' own timing constraints.
Terrain/load sensing (wheel encoders, IMU, load cells) can be consolidated under Vehicle ownership or aggregated via a dedicated Sensor Fusion submodule with clear inbound interfaces.
Tasking interface schema and command interpretation logic can be owned and maintained by Vehicle without diffusion to Teleoperation or other subsystems.

Network Link latency estimates are available to Vehicle's adaptive throttling logic with sufficient fidelity to enable stable feedback control (hysteresis and rate-limiting tuning parameters are derivable from closed-loop simulation).

Telemetry interface can export cycle-time samples, throttle state, terrain classification, and margin utilization with sufficient granularity to enable post-hoc statistical analysis and root-cause investigation.
open_risks: CRITICAL: Vehicle's real-time execution substrate is not yet specified. If Vehicle lacks a deterministic RTOS or kernel-level scheduler, DP4 realization is infeasible. Mitigation: Conduct architecture review to confirm substrate availability; if absent, revise DP4 to include substrate provisioning as a prerequisite and update project schedule/budget accordingly.

CRITICAL: SafetyAlert interface latency budget (<10 ms) is not yet validated against Vehicle's actual control-loop cycle time and Safety Services' response time. If synchronous coupling is infeasible, margin enforcement may fail under worst-case conditions. Mitigation: Measure actual SafetyAlert latency in prototype; if >10 ms, revise DP3 to include a larger safety margin buffer or implement asynchronous safety feedback with explicit margin degradation logic.

HIGH: Sensor Fusion logic for DP5 is not yet assigned to a clear module owner. If sensors are distributed across drivetrain, navigation, and payload subsystems, DP5 realization will diffuse across multiple modules, increasing integration complexity and latency. Mitigation: Conduct subsystem architecture review; consolidate sensor ownership under Vehicle or create a dedicated Sensor Fusion module with explicit inbound interfaces.

HIGH: Tasking interface schema is not yet defined. If command semantics are ambiguous or if Vehicle lacks a command parser, diffusion risk to Teleoperation increases. Mitigation: Define Tasking interface schema explicitly (e.g., JSON schema, protobuf, or equivalent); ensure Vehicle owns command interpretation logic and document translation from mission commands to internal throttling/load-balancing parameters.

MEDIUM: Adaptive throttling feedback loop stability is not yet validated. If rate-limiting and hysteresis are not carefully tuned, oscillatory behavior may occur, causing p95 cycle time to exceed CT_target. Mitigation: Implement closed-loop simulation of DP4 throttling logic with representative Network Link latency profiles; validate stability margins and document tuning parameters.

MEDIUM: Verification instrumentation is not yet specified. If Telemetry interface lacks sufficient granularity, post-hoc analysis of p95 violations may be impossible. Mitigation: Define Telemetry schema to include cycle-time samples, throttle state, terrain classification, margin utilization, and SafetyAlert events; ensure logging resolution is sufficient for statistical analysis (recommend ≥100 Hz sampling for cycle-time metrics).
evidence_needed: Architecture review of Vehicle module: confirm real-time execution substrate (RTOS, kernel-level scheduler, or equivalent) and its cycle-time guarantees. If substrate is absent, document as a prerequisite and update project plan.

Latency measurement of SafetyAlert interface: measure round-trip latency from Vehicle's margin violation detection to Safety Services' veto decision and back to Vehicle's throttling logic. Validate against <10 ms budget; if infeasible, measure actual latency and revise DP3 safety margin buffer accordingly.

Sensor ownership audit: map all load/terrain sensors (wheel encoders, IMU, load cells) to their owning subsystems (drivetrain, navigation, payload). Consolidate under Vehicle ownership or create a dedicated Sensor Fusion module with explicit inbound interfaces. Document sensor aggregation latency.

Tasking interface specification: define command schema (e.g., JSON, protobuf) and document how mission commands (target cycle time, load constraints) are translated into internal throttling and load-balancing parameters. Ensure Vehicle owns this translation logic.

Closed-loop simulation of DP4 throttling logic: implement adaptive throttling with hysteresis and rate-limiting; simulate with representative Network Link latency profiles (e.g., 50 ms mean, 100 ms p95); validate stability margins and document tuning parameters.

Telemetry interface specification: define schema to include cycle-time samples, throttle state, terrain classification, margin utilization, and SafetyAlert events. Specify logging resolution (recommend ≥100 Hz for cycle-time metrics) and data retention policy.

Prototype validation: measure actual p95 cycle time under representative duty cycles and site conditions. Validate that margin violations are reported via SafetyAlert and that Telemetry data enables root-cause analysis. If p95 > CT_target, conduct failure mode analysis and revise DP4, DP5, or DP3 as needed.
